title: Why Your Triggers Get Worse Before They Get Better in Therapy
researcherTopic: the body keeps the score, trauma and the brain
metaDescription: Discover why your trauma triggers and anxiety can feel more intense during therapy. Learn how this challenging phase is a crucial part of the healing process.
imageDescription: A soft, golden light illuminates a complex network of threads, some tangled and some newly woven into a coherent, beautiful pattern. The image evokes a sense of gentle repair and the emergence of order from chaos, representing the re-wiring of the nervous system.
finalH2: The Long, Slow Road to a Felt Sense of Safety
htmlContent:
The trauma was not the worst part. The worst part was learning to pretend it didn’t happen.

The Unraveling of a Tightly Wound Spring

It is one of the most bewildering and counter-intuitive experiences on the path of healing from complex trauma... the distinct and often alarming sense that things are getting worse just when we have finally committed to getting better. The triggers that once felt like distant thunder now feel like a storm directly overhead. The anxiety that was a low, constant hum has become a deafening roar. It’s a phenomenon I’ve witnessed in my work time and again: the moment we begin to intentionally and safely approach the wounded parts of ourselves, the system that has been holding it all together begins to protest. This is not a sign of failure. It is the first tremor of a significant and necessary earthquake, the unraveling of a spring that has been tightly wound for a lifetime. We have spent years, perhaps decades, constructing elaborate scaffolding to keep the broken pieces from collapsing, and therapy is the process of carefully dismantling that scaffolding to see what lies beneath. It is an act of immense courage, and it is almost never a comfortable one.

Every moment of genuine attention is a small act of liberation.

When the Body Begins to Speak

For so long, the body has been a silent witness, a repository for experiences the mind could not process. It has held the tension, the fear, the grief, the rage, in its muscles, its tissues, its very cells. As the renowned researcher Bessel van der Kolk has so powerfully articulated, the body keeps the score. When we enter a therapeutic space where we are finally safe enough to listen, the body begins to speak. And its first words are often a scream. The language of the body is not linear or logical; it is a language of sensation, of impulse, of imagery. The increased sensitivity, the heightened startle response, the inexplicable aches and pains - these are not new problems. They are the body’s long-suppressed testimony finally being given a voice. We are not breaking down; we are breaking open. The numbness that has been a faithful protector for so long begins to recede, and in its place, a torrent of sensation can arise. It is overwhelming, yes, but it is also the first step toward inhabiting our own lives again. For those seeking to understand this process more deeply, the National Institute of Mental Health provides extensive resources on trauma and the brain.

Consciousness doesn’t arrive. It’s what’s left when everything else quiets down.

A therapy fidget that many find soothing for restless hands is Tangle Therapy Relax (paid link).

The Necessary Disorientation of Re-Wiring

The nervous system of a person with complex trauma is a system that has been exquisitely adapted for survival in an unsafe world. It is a system on high alert, constantly scanning for threat, ready to fight, flee, or freeze at a moment’s notice. What emerges is not a pathology; it is proof of our resilience. But when we are no longer in an unsafe world, this highly attuned survival mechanism can become a source of immense suffering. The work of therapy is the work of re-wiring this system, of teaching it that the war is over. This is a slow and delicate process, and it is often deeply disorienting. As the old neural pathways begin to be pruned away and new ones are formed, there can be a sense of being untethered, of not knowing who we are without the constant hum of anxiety. We are, in a very real sense, in the liminal space between two worlds, the world of the past and the world of the possible. It is a work that requires immense patience and a willingness to be with the discomfort of not-knowing. Exploring different modalities like EMDR can offer structured ways to support this re-wiring process.

Seriously.

There’s a meaningful difference between self-improvement and self-understanding. One adds. The other reveals.

Navigating the Terrain of Parts

To survive the unsurvivable, the psyche in its wisdom often fragments into different parts. There are the parts that hold the pain, the parts that protect us from the pain, and the parts that just try to get on with daily life. For a long time, these parts may have been in conflict, working at cross-purposes, creating a sense of internal chaos. As we begin to do the work of healing, these parts can become more distinct, their voices louder, their needs more urgent. This can feel like a regression, a descent into madness, but it is actually a crucial step toward integration. We are finally beginning to meet the members of our internal family, to understand their roles, their fears, their hopes. That becomes the heart of models like Internal Family Systems, a recognition that we are not a monolithic self, but a multiplicity. The goal is not to banish the parts we don’t like, but to cultivate a relationship of compassion and curiosity with all of them. It is a path of becoming the wise and loving leader of our own internal system. You can learn more about the nature of trauma and dissociation from the International Society for the Study of Trauma and Dissociation.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link).

There’s a difference between being alone and being with yourself. One is circumstance. The other is practice.

The Long, Slow Road to a Felt Sense of Safety

Ultimately, the reason our triggers can feel worse before they get better is that we are on a work from a conceptual understanding of safety to a felt sense of it. We can tell ourselves a thousand times that we are safe, but if our body is still living in the past, the words will be meaningless. A felt sense of safety is not an idea; it is a physiological state. It is the experience of being able to take a full breath, of feeling our feet on the ground, of being able to connect with another human being without the fear of annihilation. It turns out to be not something that can be rushed or forced. It is something that is cultivated, moment by moment, through the practice of compassionate attention, through the co-regulation with a safe other, and through the slow and steady work of titration and pendulation ~ of touching into the trauma and then returning to a place of resource and ease. It is a road of exploring consciousness and the very nature of our being.

Most people don’t fear change. They fear the gap between who they were and who they haven’t become yet.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does it feel like my anxiety is getting worse in therapy?

That becomes a common experience and often a sign that you are beginning to touch into the deeper layers of your experience. As you develop a greater capacity to be with your feelings, the anxiety that was previously suppressed can come to the surface to be processed. It is a challenging but ultimately healing part of the process.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

Is it normal to feel more tired and overwhelmed?

Yes, the work of healing can be exhausting. You are basically rewiring your nervous system and processing years of stored trauma. It is important to give yourself permission to rest and to not push yourself too hard. That becomes a marathon, not a sprint.

Let that land for a moment.

How do I know if the therapy is actually working?

Look for the subtle shifts. You might notice that you are a little less reactive in certain situations, that you have a little more space between a trigger and your response, or that you are able to be a little more compassionate with yourself. These are the signs of progress, even if they feel small at first. You can also explore other perspectives on what a trauma response is to gain more insight.

What can I do to support myself through this process?

In addition to your work in therapy, it is important to cultivate practices that help you regulate your nervous system. This might include things like spending time in nature, gentle movement, or creative expression. It is also important to have a strong support system in place, whether that is friends, family, or a support group. Understanding the four F types of trauma can also provide a useful framework.

A discreet grounding tool you can stick anywhere is Calm Strips Sensory Stickers (paid link).

The path of healing is not a straight line. It is a spiral, a dance, a path of returning again and again to the places within ourselves that need our attention the most. There will be moments of genuine insight and moments of deep despair. But through it all, we are learning to become the one we have been waiting for. We are learning to be our own sanctuary.
